blocks.content: The doctor came out forty minutes later.

She was direct and not unkind. Cardiac event. Her heart had gone into a dangerous rhythm — sudden, unprovoked, related to the underlying condition. She had been unconscious but had not stopped breathing. They had stabilized her.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Is she awake?

blocks.speaker: Doctor
blocks.content: She's sedated. She'll be more alert in a few hours.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Can I see her?
The doctor looked at him.

blocks.speaker: Doctor
blocks.content: Give us another twenty minutes.
He sat back down. Thomas Hailey's hand came to his shoulder and stayed there.
Twenty-three minutes.
He walked the hallway to her room the way you walk when you are afraid of what you might find and also afraid that being afraid is using up something you need.
He paused outside the window.
She was there. On the other side of the glass. The line in her arm. The monitor blinking its steady indifferent rhythm. Too pale. Too still.
But there.
He pressed his hand flat against the glass for just a second — not even a full second — and then he pushed the door open.
The room was too bright. The sounds were wrong. All of it was wrong except for her.
He crossed to the bed. Sat in the chair. Took her hand in both of his.
He didn't say anything. He was working very hard at not falling apart in front of her.
She stirred. Her eyes opened — slowly, still surfacing. They found him.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Hey.
Her voice was rough. But it was her voice.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Hey.
She could always see through it — the pressed-down, the holding-together.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm okay.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I know.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You've been crying.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I've been working very hard at not crying, actually.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Ethan.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You can cry.
He did.

Not the managed kind. Not quietly. He put his head down and held her hand and cried the way a person cries when they have been holding something enormous for a long time and the person they were holding it for can finally see them doing it.
She didn't let go of his hand.
She kept it the whole time.
